data.title: Franklin California Intermediate-Term Tax-Free Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The municipal (muni) bond market, as measured by the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, posted a slightly negative result for the first quarter of 2025 (Q1). January and February saw positive returns supported by healthy inflows of funds and steady demand, while returns posted in March reversed those gains.
  • Detractors: Overweight allocation to muni bonds with 20 or more years to maturity, security selection in A rated bonds and underweight allocation to AAA rated bonds.
  • Contributors: Overweight allocation to muni bonds with no external credit rating, security selection in AAA rated bonds and overweight allocation to muni bonds with two years to maturity.
  • Outlook: Potential policy changes under US President Donald Trump’s administration, such as the possible elimination of tax exemptions for certain bonds and the cancellation of federal leases, are adding uncertainty to the muni market outlook, alongside broader concerns about how federal decisions may impact local economies.

Performance Review

Over the quarter, Franklin California Intermediate-Term Tax-Free Income Fund underperformed its benchmark, the Linked Bloomberg Muni M.F. CA Intermediate/10-Year Muni Bond Index. The fund’s yield curve positioning detracted from relative performance during the first quarter, primarily driven by our overweight to muni bonds with 20 or more years to maturity and underweight to muni bonds with five years to maturity. However, this was partially offset by our overweight to muni bonds with two years to maturity and underweight to muni bonds with 10 years to maturity, which limited some of the losses. Rating allocations benefited relative returns for the period, led by our overweight to bonds with no external credit rating and underweight to AA rated bonds. Conversely, our underweight to AAA rated bonds partially mitigated some of those gains. Security selection detracted from relative results, particularly due to selection in A and AA rated securities. Meanwhile, selection in AAA rated securities added to returns.

Outlook

There was a significant swing in market sentiment across Q1 as participants digested the first few months of the new Trump administration. January saw positive excess returns across a number of sectors as market sentiment moved higher in anticipation of new business friendly initiatives. This was replaced by a negative shift in risk appetite during February and March as unclear tariff policies became the market’s focus. Throughout the quarter, investment-grade munis underperformed US Treasuries. Despite modest outflows in March, muni funds overall benefited from steady inflows earlier in the quarter. Muni credit fundamentals remain broadly stable, with signs of improvement even in sectors that struggled post-pandemic, though revenue growth is moderating, and economic tailwinds are slowing. High reserves and fiscal flexibility continue to support most issuers, and credit ratings are expected to remain steady over the next six months, though sector-specific challenges persist. Potential policy changes under the Trump administration, such as the possible elimination of tax exemptions for certain bonds and the cancellation of federal leases, are adding uncertainty to the muni market outlook, alongside broader concerns about how federal decisions may impact local economies. At the same time, US consumer sentiment is softening, and overall uncertainty around the economic growth outlook has increased in response to the administration’s proposed reforms and policy direction. However, hard data continues to indicate a resilient US economy. The labor market appears to have reached an equilibrium of a low level of both new additions and layoffs, with a still-low unemployment rate, and economic activity remains solid. Going forward, our economists are looking to deregulation and tax reforms to support growth over the medium term. The heightened uncertainty surrounding the administration’s trade policy and the impact on US businesses makes US Federal Reserve policy difficult to predict. It remains to be seen whether risks are skewed toward a significant slowdown in growth or higher inflation. However, our economists remain optimistic on US growth, considering that the economy remains on solid footing and pro-growth measures are still expected. Consequently, we are penciling in only one 25-basis point rate reduction in 2025, though an absence of rate cuts this year is also possible.

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit franklintempleton.com.

The fund began offering Advisor Class shares on 10/31/2008 and the fund began offering A Class shares on 9/10/2018. Performance quotations have been calculated as follows: (a) for Advisor Class periods prior to 10/31/2008, a restated figure is used based on the fund’s Class A1 performance; for A Class periods prior to 9/10/2018, a restated figure is used based on the fund’s Class A1 performance. The performance was adjusted to take into account differences in class-specific operating expenses and maximum sales charges. (b) For periods after share class offering, performance for the specific share class is used, reflecting the expenses and maximum sales charges applicable to that class.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect contractual fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements, which cannot be terminated prior to 10/31/2025 without Board consent. Additional amounts may be voluntarily waived and/or reimbursed and may be modified or discontinued at any time without notice.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. Portfolios focused on a single state are subject to greater risk of adverse economic and regulatory changes in that state than a geographically diversified fund. Changes in the credit rating of a bond, or in the credit rating or financial strength of a bond’s issuer, insurer or guarantor, may affect the bond’s value. An investor may be subject to the federal Alternative Minimum Tax, and state and local taxes may apply. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

A basis point (bp, or bps) is one one-hundredth of one percent (1/100% or 0.01%).

The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index is a broad measure of the municipal bond market with maturities of at least one year. Source: Bloomberg Indices. The yield curve shows the relationship between yields and maturity dates for a similar class of bonds.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The Fund’s benchmark is the Bloomberg Municipal M.F. CA Intermediate Index. The Linked Bloomberg Muni M.F. CA Intermediate/10-Year Muni Bond Index reflects performance of the Bloomberg 10-Year Municipal Bond Index from the fund’s inception through 11/29/1993 and the Bloomberg Municipal M.F. CA Intermediate Index thereafter.The Bloomberg Municipal M.F.

CA Intermediate Index measures the performance of investment-grade California municipal bonds with maturities of at least five years and less than 10 years. Source: Bloomberg Indices.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

Effective Duration is a duration calculation for bonds with embedded options. Effective duration takes into account that expected cash flows will fluctuate as interest rates change. Duration measures the sensitivity of price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. The higher the duration number, the more sensitive a fixed-income investment will be to interest rate changes. The 30-day SEC yield is calculated using the net income (interest and dividends) per share earned over a trailing 30-day period (annualized), divided by the fund’s share price at the end of that period. It may not equal the fund’s actual income distribution rate, which reflects the fund’s past dividends paid to shareholders.

Morningstar Rating™: Source: Morningstar®, 03/31/2025. For each mutual fund and ETF with at least a 3-year history, Morningstar calculates a Morningstar Rating™ based on how a fund ranks on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure against other funds in the same category. This measure takes into account variations in a fund’s monthly performance, and does not take into account the effects of sales charges and loads, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of funds in each category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. The fund’s Advisor Class shares received a Morningstar Rating of 4, 4 and 3 and fund’s Class A shares received a Morningstar Rating of 3, 3 and 3 star(s) for the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. Franklin Templeton provides this fund’s Morningstar Rating™ for Advisor Class and Class A shares only. Other share classes may have different Morningstar ratings. Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC. © Franklin Templeton. All rights reserved.

Before investing, carefully consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. You can find this and other information in each prospectus, or summary prospectus, if available, at www.franklintempleton.com. Please read it carefully.

Morningstar Rating™

Overall Rating as of March 31, 2025

★★★★(4-Star) Advisor Class

★★★(3-Star) Class A

As of 03/31/2025 the fund’s Advisor Class shares received a 4 star and Class A shares received a 3 star overall Morningstar rating™, measuring risk-adjusted returns against 58, 57 and 46 Muni California Intermediate funds over the 3-, 5- and 10- year periods, respectively. A fund’s overall rating is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) rating metrics.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Blue Owl Capital Corporation: Fairly Valued, Ahead Of Q1
data.description:
Blue Owl Capital Corporation (NYSE:OBDC) is the second-largest publicly traded BDC, with first quarter results coming out soon. Amid recent market volatility, investments known for their dividends and income are a strategy for strong total returns in a potential bear market.

In the case of OBDC, I think it’s fairly valued, unlikely to suffer much in capital losses but with a portfolio that makes it less likely to provide a good yield for dividend reinvestment on its current price.

Dividend History

OBDC has been around for about five years. During that time, it’s delivered on it mission of paying dividends.

We see here that it's paid every quarter. While distributions initially declined, they began to increase again after interest rates increased, increasing the regular dividend and separate, special dividends that began to occur quarterly as well.

For income investors, there's scarcely been a bad time to buy, with yields on cost spanning 10% to 15% throughout this period.

Portfolio and Strategy

What explains their dividend? Like many BDCs, the company primarily invests its capital through loan origination in private companies. Recently, they reported $17.4B in investments, of which 83% was senior secured loans, with 96% of their loans carrying floating rates. These floating rates explain the increased distributions, following the Fed's hike regimen.

About 10% of the portfolio is in common equity. This is a bit of an exception that proves the rule as most of this is ownership in businesses that do other forms of lending.

The portfolio is heavily diversified across many industries. Management has a preference toward industries with defensive characteristics. This reflects why software and insurance are their two biggest concentrations. OBDC seeks the respective leaders in these industries as an additional means to reduce their overall risk.

To date, such a strategy seems to be paying off on the risk management side. Above, see consolidated data for OBDC (which merged with OBDE in January). Non-accruals have remained less than 0.5%, in spite of challenges borrowers with floating-rate loans have faced across credit markets.

Future Outlook

While I remain confident in OBDC's ability to manage its risk going forward, I'm not convinced there's a lot of upside, both as the strategy and the current share price are concerned.

One of the issues I have is their focus on the upper middle market among portfolio companies. The appeal of such clients is initially easy to understand. Following the merger with OBDE, OBDC is now the second-largest BDC. With so much capital, it's not surprising that they'd go for the larger businesses, which should allow for larger deals.

Another BDC I like (as well as the one I've followed the longest) has a different perspective: PennantPark Floating Rate Capital (PFLT). Their slide above shows some key differences between the upper and core middle markets, core being PFLT's client base. The most obvious advantage is the higher coupons core loans have compared to upper, as well as more debt coverage, more frequent equity co-investments, and less competition with other lenders.

Above, I've a posted comparison of the total returns of both BDCs for the last five years. OBDC's is 86%; PFLT's is 159%. If one's looking at a BDC investing in senior secured loans, I think there's a clear difference in results by now. This lower general coupon is concerning, and management's remarks during the Q4 earnings call weren't reassuring:

Next, I want to take a moment to look back on what we accomplished in 2024. Overall, the year presented some challenges. M&A deal flow was disappointing, elevated base rates led to a focus on credit risk, and a strong, broadly syndicated loan market resulted in spread compression.

Subsequent remarks observed that M&A activity across potential clients was light, and even refinancing volumes were down. With Fed rate cuts having begun last September (cuts that management indicated have trickled into 70% of the portfolio), there's a lot more room for continued squeeze of net interest income on the portfolio. With Fed rate cuts looming in the future, it's likely that this dividend yield supported by floating rates will face a trim in the near future.

ROE guidance for 2025 was just 10%. As much as they touted Blue Owl's extensive networks, expertise, and origination platform, I'm not seeing a lot of upside being realized from this. If the principal is secure (which I think it is), then the main chance for some upside is a discount to NAV.

The chart above does show that OBDC trades at a slight discount to NAV (expressed as Price/Book). Discounts, however, are common, while premiums are rare. In my view, anyone hoping to benefit from capital gains needs a discount of 20% or more.

Otherwise, with only a nominal discount of 10% and a 12% yield that is braced for some trimming, I get the impression that OBDC is fairly valued. As Q1 results approach on May 8th, we'll need to look for positive signs that could change this.

The most likely would be better-than-expected improvements from the merger with OBDE, which combined their portfolios. $5M in operational synergies were already projected, but other savings are less clear. If management can highlight some of these, perhaps this will improve the net income and mitigate some potential dividend loss.

Conclusion

OBDC is a decent BDC. Its portfolio isn't one where I imagine investors would lose their shirts. It's also not one that invests in senior secured loans with the highest-return characteristics. Folks looking to achieve strong total returns in the face of a bear market need the yield to make that reinvestment effective. At today's prices and with a disappointing outlook, I'm not convinced OBDC offers that. That is why I rate it a Hold for now.

data.title: Amid The Noise, Active Management Quietly Reinvents Itself
data.description:
By Clare Flynn Levy

Despite the headlines proclaiming its demise, active investment management is not going extinct — it’s evolving. The traditional mutual fund may be fading, but active decision-making now shines through new channels: model portfolios, direct indexing, and self-service apps. Whether it’s a retail investor fine-tuning a separately managed account (SMA), an advisor allocating across ETFs, or an endowment selecting specialty managers to meet diverse investment policy requirements, the index is no longer the boundary between passive and active — it’s the starting point for active decision-making.

Investment management is, after all, decision-making as a service. What’s changing is who (or what) is making which decisions, what tools are being used to make them, and how those decisions — and their results — are being delivered to end clients. While traditional active mutual funds have indeed seen significant outflows — $432 billion in the 12 months to 31 March 2025 — those dollars haven’t vanished from the market. According to Morningstar’s US Fund Flows research, they’ve largely rotated into passive vehicles, which took in $568 billion over the same period. On the surface, that shift supports the “passive takeover” narrative. But it actually reflects a reconfiguration of where and how active choices are being expressed.

Beneath the Surface

The packaging of active decision-making has evolved beyond the traditional mutual fund. Compelling trading apps combined with near-zero transaction costs have led to a boom in self-directed investing that, as Broadridge’s 2024 US Investor Pulse study points out, spans all generational cohorts. These self-directed investors increasingly focus on ETFs and direct equities rather than mutual funds.

Meanwhile, as of June 2024, 79% of US equity investors maintained an investment relationship with a financial advisor. These advised assets are also shifting from mutual funds to ETFs and direct equities, facilitated by the proliferation of SMAs and unified managed accounts (UMAs). SMAs, in particular, offer individual investors unprecedented levels of access, transparency, and tax efficiency through strategies like tax-loss harvesting. In other countries, the trend is the same: self-service and personalization of investment solutions at scale.

Either way, someone — or something — is making active decisions.

The self-directed investor wants hands-on control. They are active by definition, but are not willing to pay a third party for the decision-making. Implicitly, they either believe they can outperform professionals, they value the entertainment of market participation enough not to care, or both.

The advice-channel investor, conversely, has outsourced decision-making to their financial advisor, trusting that a professional will deliver better outcomes. Financial advisors have never been more scalable as a business, partly because they can easily outsource the actual investment decisions to an expanding universe of model portfolios, ranging from strategic asset allocation models to tactical thematic strategies to risk-targeted solutions. These portfolios contain the same active decision-making found in mutual funds, just without the trade execution services.

Institutional allocators continue to value alpha and will pay for it. As indexes have become increasingly concentrated, these sophisticated investors are turning back to active managers for diversification. But today’s allocators are less easily seduced by past performance; they demand evidence of skill.

The industry is responding to these changes. Active equity portfolio managers, driven by cost-cutting imperatives, are reevaluating the division of labor within their investment teams. Product strategists are increasingly evaluating quant and fundamental strategies side-by-side, applying fresh eyes to the consolidation of multi-brand product ranges. In leading firms, formerly siloed investment teams are being integrated to foster collaboration and cross-pollination of ideas. This approach emphasizes decision-making quality, regardless of whether the signal originates from human insight or an algorithm.

Key Takeaway

Surface-level data suggests that active fund management is an industry in retreat: dollars flowing out of active funds and into passive alternatives. But under the surface, active decision-making is more widespread, more diversified, and more structurally embedded in the investment landscape than ever before. The imperative for active managers is no longer preservation, but adaptation. In a marketplace that demands personalization, transparency, and demonstrable value, relevance depends on embracing new delivery mechanisms and decision-making frameworks. The future of active investing will be shaped by those who evolve with it — quietly, strategically, and decisively.

Disclaimer: Please note that the content of this site should not be construed as investment advice, nor do the opinions expressed necessarily reflect the views of CFA Institute.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Sysco: Serving Up Steady Growth In Uncertain Times
data.description:
Sysco (NYSE:SYY) presents an intriguing opportunity for investors as it is a steady, reliable and rationally traded business in a market where volatility and unpredictability have been the norm. As a market leader in the foodservice business domestically and internationally, Sysco has the brand recognition and delivery network to continue to grow market share and earnings even if it were to simply rely upon its existing infrastructure and business momentum. Thankfully, Sysco has not been content to simply rest on its laurels, as the business continually invests in addressing the pain points of customers by implementing new and innovative initiatives such as its SYGMA platform specializing in servicing the largest national restaurant chains and the newly conceived Sysco To Go cash and carry channel. Unfortunately, the main drawback of Sysco's business lies in the nature of the foodservice business, as despite being a solid company in the consumer staples sector Sysco is more vulnerable to earnings drawdowns in recessionary conditions than other defensively positioned equities.

A Dominant Player with Room to Grow

There is very little not to like about Sysco's operations and financial trajectory in recent years. During the COVID pandemic, the company was nearly left for dead as it was believed that the foodservice industry would never truly recover, but in truth Sysco seized the day by expanding its market footprint and the diversity of its product offerings. In just the past few years, Sysco made several accretive acquisitions by buying out smaller players at discount prices, including Greco and Sons in 2021 to expand into the Italian specialty products market, Edward Don and Company in 2023 to expand into the foodservice equipment and supplies business and BIX Produce in 2023 to expand its grab and go offerings and grow into areas of the Midwest the company previously did not serve. Unlike most companies engaging in such acquisition activities, however, Sysco managed during the same period to deleverage its business substantially with strong cash flows driving the debt to EBITDA ratio from 5.15x in 2021 to 2.80x as of the most recent earnings presentation. With all that said, due to the extremely fragmented nature of the foodservice business, even solid progress can be a slow grind, as illustrated by the fact that Sysco's revenue, earnings, and US foodservice market share only improved by 6.6%, 10% and 1% respectively since 2023.

Thankfully, the leadership team at Sysco has not been content to simply acquire smaller companies and allow the extremely slow growth momentum of the existing business to stand on its own. Sysco has been continually investing in initiatives to distinguish their services from competitors and providing value-added benefits to their customers. For example, Sysco's SYGMA platform provides a technological solution for larger customers ensuring they have timely orders that include only the products they need, when they need them at the best possible prices, leveraging the cost benefits of consistent high-volume purchasing. The value added by SYGMA is undeniable when considering that in the most recent quarter, which otherwise yielded flat revenue growth, SYGMA was a bright spot with 9.5% sales growth. Expanding this concept of offering lower cost solutions as options to foodservice customers, Sysco has begun ramping an effort to open cash and carry locations labeled Sysco To Go. These locations allow Sysco to compete with membership clubs such as Costco and BJ's by offering smaller restaurant owners the ability to purchase supplies directly and in bulk, saving on delivery costs and credit card fees. With Sysco To Go in its early stages with only one location in the Houston area, this is yet another exciting opportunity for the company to continue its dominance and grow market share as the foodservice business continually matures and consolidates.

Risks - Defensive or Discretionary?

One of the challenges inherent in Sysco's business is that while the company trades as a defensive equity in the consumer staples sector to backstop portfolios against economic downturns, the reality is that the foodservice business is fairly vulnerable under recessionary conditions. As research has shown, consumers tend to trade down from full-service restaurants to fast-food and other low-cost options when recessionary indicators such as GDP and unemployment trend unfavorably. While it is true that Sysco serves customers spanning the entire industry, including full-service and limited-service restaurants, the net effect is that Sysco's revenues and earnings are likely to decline more than its sector assignment may suggest since sales from lower-cost customers may effectively cannibalize sales from premium ones. This trend has already begun to be realized via decreased foot traffic and lowered guidance from the company for 2025.

Valuation

For the past four years or so, Sysco has been typically traded in a range between $70-$90 and, consequently, has been a better trading stock than a long-term investment during that time. However, despite this, the business has continued to grow and, as a result, at the lower band of the range where the stock is currently trading it has a very reasonable valuation at less than 0.5x price to sales and roughly 16x price to earnings. Additionally, the stock offers a 3% yield at current prices after one of the best dividend raises in years, a 6% increase from $0.51 per share to $0.54 per share last month. While diluted earnings per share have receded slightly year-over-year from $0.85 per share to $0.82 per share in the most recent quarter, the company regardless guides for at least 1% EPS growth for the full year. With a recently increased buyback authorization and a history of consistently reducing the outstanding share count (most recently from ~502 million a year ago to ~489 million as of the end of March), expectations of continued modest earnings growth seem very realistic. At $70 or under per share, the stock is a very solid buy as a steady, low-beta anchor with the potential for substantial upside in the medium term as earnings continue to slowly compound to the upside.

Conclusion

While it is true that Sysco has not been a market-beating investment in recent years, as it has been overshadowed by high-flying, high-growth names amidst euphoric market sentiment, it may be the best medicine for investors who are uneasy about increasingly concerning economic data and the high level of uncertainty created by tariff risk. Sysco is a solid, reliable, slow-growing company that is as inevitable as it is boring - it is unlikely to make you rich, but you can invest with confidence that your money is in good hands. The illustrious Warren Buffett, who is finally retiring after decades of market-beating success, would likely say that Sysco is the proverbial "wonderful company at a fair price". With this in mind, I feel very comfortable recommending Sysco as a BUY.

data.title: U.S. Bank Stocks Weather April Tariff Storms
data.description:
US bank stocks recovered from April's tariff storms, during which they faced one of their worst weeks since 1990.

The potential impact of higher tariffs halted a sector rally ahead of first quarter earnings reports. Bank stocks crashed April 3, with severe losses spilling over into the next day. The sector followed a broader market trend and traded up for the rest of the month. The 212 banks in an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis recorded a median total return of negative 3.2% for April, underperforming the S&P 500's 0.7% decline.

Lower stock prices translated to lower valuations. The median price-to-adjusted tangible book value (TBV) for the 212 banks was 126.1% as of April 30, down from 134.6% at March 31 and 140.3% at the end of 2024.

Least expensive banks

Dallas-based First Foundation Inc. (FFWM) was the lowest-valued bank in the analysis by price-to-adjusted TBV for the fourth month in a row. Its valuation was 45.9% at the end of April, and would be higher if preferred shares from a capital raise in July 2024 were converted to common stock. The conversion would have lowered the bank's basic TBV to $9.42 from $11.77 at March 31, according to an April 30 investor presentation.

First Foundation recorded net income of $6.9 million in the first quarter, following net losses in the previous two quarters. The bank's net interest margin rose for the fourth consecutive quarter, and management is projecting continued expansion. With a return to profitability and a smaller balance sheet, First Foundation's capital ratios increased across the board on a quarter-over-quarter basis.

BCB Bancorp Inc. (BCBP) was the only other bank in the analysis that ended April trading at less than half of adjusted TBV. For the first quarter, the Bayonne, New Jersey-based bank reported an $8.8 million net loss available to common stockholders, including preferred dividends, largely due to a spike in the loan loss provision. In its first quarter earnings release, the bank cited a specific reserve of $13.7 million for a $34.2 million credit in the cannabis sector.

"Although the borrower remains current, the significant deterioration in their financial condition warranted a downgrade to non-accrual status and the establishment of the reserve," Michael Shriner, President and CEO of BCB Bancorp, said in the release.

The third-cheapest bank, Eagle Bancorp Inc. (EGBN), is grappling with asset quality issues in the commercial real estate office and government contractor sectors. The Bethesda, Maryland-based bank, with a valuation at 50.0% of adjusted TBV as of April 30, continued building its reserve for loan losses and eked out a profit of $1.7 million in the first quarter.

Franchise finance and small business loans were stress points again for First Internet Bancorp (INBK), the fourth-lowest valued bank. It was the worst market performer in the analysis, with shares plunging 20.8% the day after the first quarter earnings release and ending the month with a total return of negative 20.6%.

The ninth-lowest bank by valuation, Los Angeles-based RBB Bancorp (RBB), announced the retirement of CEO David Morris.

Most expensive banks

Dewitt, New York-based Community Financial System Inc. (CBU) traded at the highest price-to-adjusted TBV in the analysis for the third straight month. Its valuation was 343.3% at April 30, down from 391.2% at the end of March.

Community Financial disclosed in its first quarter earnings report that a $59.7 million sequential decline in the consumer indirect portfolio led to total loans falling $11.2 million, or 0.1%. President and CEO Dimitar Karaivanov, in an April 29 earnings conference call, described indirect auto lending as a "wildcard" given aggressive competition and tariff impacts.

While Citigroup Inc. (C) was the 14th-cheapest bank in the analysis, the other three members of the Big 4 US banks slotted into the upper echelon. JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) ended April as the ninth-highest valued bank, followed by Bank of America Corp. (BAC) in 14th and Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC) in 16th. The four are among the 15 largest banks in the world by total assets.

Investment banking revenue at JPMorgan and Bank of America beat Visible Alpha estimates in the first quarter because of robust activity in debt underwriting. Citigroup topped its total investment bank fee estimate primarily from higher-than-expected advisory fees.

Following first quarter earnings, analysts projected even higher debt underwriting fees for all three banks in 2025 while slashing estimates for equity underwriting. The advisory fee estimates were mixed: down for JPMorgan and Bank of America but up for Citigroup.

Analysts also have been cutting loan growth estimates for most of the largest US banks, including JPMorgan, Citigroup and Wells Fargo. An outlier in the opposite direction is Bank of America; its net loan growth estimate for 2025 increased 84 basis points from March 31 to April 29.

About this analysis

S&P Global Market Intelligence analyzed US banks trading on the Nasdaq, NYSE or NYSE American with total assets of greater than $3 billion. The analysis excludes banks in the mutual holding company ownership structure and other operating subsidiaries.

Adjusted tangible book value is calculated as the sum of tangible common equity, loss reserves and unrealized gain or loss from held-to-maturity securities, tax-adjusted at the 21% corporate rate, less nonperforming assets and loans 90 or more days past due but still accruing interest, divided by common shares outstanding.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ACIO: Doing Its Job In A Tough Equity Market (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

We last covered the Aptus Collared Investment Opportunity ETF (BATS:ACIO) last year, when we favored this fund over an outright S&P 500 position. The name has delivered since, presenting a total return closely mirroring the index, but with a much shallower drawdown during the recent April 2025 market swoon.

We are going to revisit this name and its composition, and highlight why it is a robust 'Hold' for investors looking for equity exposure, but tilting more conservatively.

The ETF is meant to buffer the downside

Let us look again at the fund objective and build, and see how it actually performed versus its parameters:

ACIO was designed to capture additional income, giving up some upside but using a portion of the proceeds to minimize downside even more. It's an actively-managed ETF in a tax efficient wrapper, managing a basket of options each month to pursue a goal of 65% upside: 50% downside capture relative to the S&P 500.

Before we get into the actual structural aspects, let us see the fund performance this year:

While the year to date price change is similar, please note that the S&P 500 had an almost -20% drawdown this year (moved from almost +5% to almost -15%), while ACIO had only a -12% one roughly. Furthermore, at the lowest point in April, SPY was down -15% for the year, while ACIO was down only -9%. The fund performed largely in line with expectations and its goals during these difficult times.

As we highlighted before, ACIO is a conservative choice when it comes to equity exposure because it provides for lower drawdowns during recessions. When investors want to be defensive, they either sell stocks or move into lower beta instruments. ACIO is such an instrument, and we saw that it performed as expected.

The fund does well during recessions

While we do not have extensive historic data due to the fund being fairly new (data exists starting with 2020), we can nonetheless see its performance in 2022, when it recorded a rough -50% drawdown relative to the S&P 500:

The name was down only -12% versus -20% for the SPY, and more importantly, never had very deep drawdowns exceeding -13%. This is important to note because investors tend to panic during recessions, and some make the mistake of selling at the bottom. Buying into a more conservative fund like ACIO ensures that losses are buffered, and that a bear market does not wreck a portfolio.

How is the fund structured to achieve this pay-off profile?

The ETF contains 82 stocks that mimic the S&P 500 sectoral allocation and the largest components. The fund manager then overlays covered calls on individual names mainly, and buys SPY puts. We are going to have a closer look at the SPY downside protection shortly. However, it is worth noting that the fund does not pursue a buy-write strategy where it generates a high dividend yield. No. The cash from the sold calls is used to prop-up the NAV, and limit the downside. This is a defensive way to use covered calls.

On the downside protection side, let us look at what the fund actually does:

We have taken out the holdings from the fund website, and identified the index options. Please note that the SPX ticker is the one for the S&P 500 index, whereas in our article we also use interchangeably the SPY ticker, which is for the largest ETF tracking the index. The fund therefore does options on the index directly.

The option structures currently on the books are:

  • Long call spread 5800/6000: the fund is long 1200 contracts of the 5800 strike call, and short 1200 contracts of the 6000 strike call. This structure ensures some upside is captured if the market rallies back up.
  • Long put spread 5400/4800: the fund is long 3200 contracts of the 5400 strike put, and short 3200 contracts of the 4800 strike put. This is the main protection, and it has a June 20, 2025 expiry.
  • The fund is also long a 5800 call with a 5/30 expiry. This outright long ensures upside participation

Kindly note that the fund manager runs the portfolio deltas constantly, and has the ability to see the net delta of the overall portfolio, including options. Deltas are then utilized to adjust or roll option positions.

The takeaway here is that this is an actively managed fund which uses options to buffer the downside and participate in most of the upside, rather than generate buy-write yield. The name has done its job so far in 2025, and we expect it to continue to do so.

Analytics

  • AUM: $1.8 bil
  • Yield: 0.48%
  • Expense ratio: 0.79%
  • Sharpe ratio: 0.47
  • St Dev: 11.12 (3Y)

Fund forward

ACIO is a long position in equities, are more specifically in the S&P 500. If the overall market goes down, ACIO will follow, although with a significant buffer, as we have seen. There is no other 'magic' here, with options being used to keep the NAV high if the market moves lower. The ideal case for ACIO in 2025 is a range bound market, where the fund can monetize some of the high volatility via writing covered calls.

If the market rallies upwards, ACIO will participate in that rally, thus maintaining a high degree of the upside here. We like this name because it offers a collared approach on the downside, therefore not collapsing when the entire market is significantly lower. This feature allows a rational investor to actually buy equities at the bottom, rather than panic sell. If investors feel we are due for a vicious bear market, then they can cut their overall equity exposure more. ACIO will move lower if the bear market materializes, but it will do so to a much lesser degree than the index.

Conclusion

ACIO is an equity exchange-traded fund. The name employs covered calls and put spreads to buffer the downside, while retaining a large percentage of the S&P 500 upside during rallies. We started covering the name last year with a 'Buy' rating, arguing the fund was a better choice than SPY. The name has delivered this year, having a drawdown of roughly 50% when compared to the index, and continues to be a robust hold for a bear market. We are downgrading the fund because the name will lose value if a true bear market materializes, since ACIO represents ultimately a long equities exposure. We still favor ACIO over SPY.

data.title: General Dynamics: A Rare Blend Of Stability, Attractive Dividend And Value
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Owing to the complex economic landscape marked by geopolitical tensions and shifting market dynamics, investors have to navigate the headwinds. As investors navigate these challenges, I find General Dynamics Corporation (NYSE:GD) to be a resilient investment opportunity. The company’s diversified portfolio, strong financial performance and market position offer an interesting opportunity for long-term investors. Perhaps, those seeking stability, growth, and income. It is against this backdrop that I rate this stock a buy.

Financial Performance & Growth Momentum

GD has exemplified consistent financial strength, underpinned by solid revenue growth and consistent profitability.

While the above chart demonstrates its resilient financial performance historically, its most recent performance cements it. In Q1 2025, the company reported $12.22 billion in revenue, 13.9% YoY growth. It was the fourth consecutive quarter of double-digit revenue growth and at least the 10th successive quarter of positive revenue growth. Its EPS was $3.66 beating Wall Street estimates by about 5.7%, and growing by more than 27% YoY. Net income grew to approximately $994 million, reflecting a 23% improvement compared to the previous year.

These results depict the company’s ability to deliver double-digit growth across its key metrics. As such, it signifies the company’s strong operational execution and demand for its products and services. Given its book-to-bill ratio of 0.8x and a total contract value of more than $141 billion, this company is well positioned for sustained revenue growth.

Its diversity played a crucial role in its performance. The aerospace segment had a stand-out performance with 45.2% and 59.4% in revenue and operating earnings growth. This was driven by higher aircraft production. Further, the marine systems had its revenue growing by 7.7% to about $3.6 billion, with both technologies and combat systems segments registering 6.8% and 3.5% revenue growth, respectively.

Comparatively, its financials are not only the best amongst its peers, but also the most stable. This speaks to its resilience and consistency relative to its peers.

In general, GD continues to exhibit resilience and upward momentum in its financials, which suggests a strong demand for its offerings. The strong order backlog at $88.7 billion in Q1 2025 and the estimated contract value of more than $141 billion points to a sustainable financial growth in the future.

Positioned To Leverage Geopolitical Tensions

Around the globe, the military budget has been on the rise, driven by ongoing geopolitical differences. In 2024, the budget reaches a record high of about $2.5 trillion, marking more than 7% YoY growth.

This rising expenditure is substantially dedicated to modernizing military capabilities, and I think GD stands to benefit from this trend. Its portfolio which includes Virginia-Class submarines, Gulfstream tanks, Abrams tanks and Stryker vehicles aligns directly with the modernization need.

GD’s recent $12.4 billion contract award for its Virginia-Class submarine underscores its pivotal role in the US naval modernization, which is one of the highest spenders on military in the world. The contract combines with a backlog of orders across the defense segment, providing revenue visibility for years to come.

As opposed to cyclical industries, defense spending tends to remain resilient during economic downturns as national security stands to be a priority. This company’s exposure to long-term government contracts improves cash flow stability, making this company a defensive investment in an uncertain economic climate. Given the increasing recession odds, I think it’s time to consider this stock.

Dividend Aristocrat With Shareholder Appeal?

If you are an investor focusing on income, GD offers a compelling story. The company has a 30-year track record of consecutive dividend growth, compared to the sector median of 2.5 years and 33 years of consecutive dividend payments.

Its 10-year dividend CAGR of 8.49% is more than 18% higher than the sector median of 7.18%. Above all, its yield is significantly higher than the sector median, making it more attractive than its peers.

Most importantly, with a dividend payout ratio of 46.49% TTM, its dividend policy is highly sustainable and has ample room for growth. I am delighted by the company's capital allocation. For instance, in Q1 2025, the company spend $383 million on dividend payments, $600 million in share repurchase and $142 million in capital expenditure. This disciplined capital allocation demonstrates a commitment to balancing growth and shareholder returns.

Tenacity Amid Policy Uncertainty

The economic climate in 2025 is characterized by tariff concerns, inflation and potential slowdowns as shown by US economic contraction in Q1 2025.

Although these factors have led to volatility in the stock market, GD’s defensive nature has offered a buffer. Its reliance on government contracts insulates it from consumer driven economic cycles, and its global customer base mitigates risks associated with US-specific policy changes.

Its tenacity is shown by the 3.28% stock gains YTD, compared to S&P’s 3.93% loss. I think this further demonstrates investors’ confidence in the company’s fundamentals.

Valuation

From a comparative PE valuation approach, GD is undervalued. With a trailing GAAP PE of 18.95 which is below the sector median of 22.67 and a forward PE ratio of 18.31 compared to the sector median of 20.32, it follows that this stock is undervalued. This is not justifiable when I look at its diluted EPS YoY growth of 17.51% which is above the sector median of 5.90% and its forward diluted EPS growth rate of 11.56% compared to the sector median of 8.09%.

In my opinion, these figures point out that GD should even trade at a premium. However, I estimate my price target using the projected EPS for 2025 ($14.92), and the sector median average forward PE of 20.32. I am adopting this PE because I expect the market to at least take this undervalued stock at par with its peers given its strong fundamentals highlighted in this article. These assumptions land me to a price estimate of about $303.17, translating to an 11.4% upside potential.

Risks

My bullish take isn’t without risks; some of the risks to my thesis are:

  • Government spending changes: In case government expenditure on defense is cut, this could result in lower demand, potentially hurting topline growth.
  • Geopolitical uncertainty: Although tensions drive demand, de-escalation could reduce the need for defense spending.

Conclusion

General Dynamics is a rare blend of defensive stability, attractive dividend, and a positive value gap. While there are risks worth watching, this company remains a buy in 2025 and beyond given the tough economic outlook and the company’s resilience in such times.

data.title: Tariff Uncertainty Clouds Long-Term Outlook For Tech Companies
data.description:
Tech companies are exploring short-term strategies to navigate global tariff uncertainty while holding off on long-term decisions about manufacturing and expansion.

It has been more than a month since the Trump administration announced a sweeping range of tariffs on imports from scores of countries, but executives and industry experts remain unclear on what duties will be in effect in the months and quarters to come. US President Donald Trump has imposed 145% tariffs on imports from China, while most other countries are subject to 10% tariffs amid a 90-day pause announced April 9. China has retaliated by raising tariffs on US goods to 125%, though Washington and Beijing have some exemptions in place for semiconductors. US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said the exemption is temporary, with chips and other electronics set to be covered under new sector-specific tariffs that will be announced in the near future.

The lack of clarity is complicating the outlook for tech companies, with multiple executives citing a lack of visibility beyond the second quarter. Longer term, expectations are low for the potential of major gains for US manufacturing to be realized in the next four years.

"There's a reluctance to move ahead, even with well-laid-out plans, until the whole trade framework [becomes] guidelines," said Stavros Gadinis, a professor and faculty director at the University of California's Berkeley Center for Law and Business. "This is an extreme reversal of times for the tech industry, and something that the industry was definitely not ready to deal with. This element of surprise is a big problem for companies."

Tariff troubles

Among the companies to feel the biggest impact from tariffs is Apple Inc. (AAPL), which has a well-established global supply chain. Although the company has taken steps to diversify away from mainland China, a significant portion of its manufacturing occurs in markets including India, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan and Vietnam.

To minimize the impact of the US tariffs on Chinese imports, Apple CEO Tim Cook told analysts this month that the majority of iPhones sold in the US in the June quarter would be built in India. Meanwhile, almost all iPads, Macs, Apple Watches and AirPods products sold in the US will have Vietnam as their country of origin. "China would continue to be the country of origin for the vast majority of total product sales outside the US," Cook said on the company's May 1 earnings call.

Although iPhones and other consumer electronics are exempt from the 145% tariffs placed on Chinese imports, they are subject to the 20% rate that Trump has tied to the international drug trade, particularly fentanyl.

"We estimate the impact, assuming that the current global tariff rates, policies and applications don't change for the balance of the quarter, to be $900 million to our costs," Cook said. "I wouldn't want to predict the mix of production in the future ... but I wanted to give you clarity for the June quarter of where the country of origins are so you can use that for your modeling."

Consumer sentiment from 451 Research's 2025 Macroeconomic Outlook, Consumer Spending, Sentiment & Expectations survey revealed that 60% of respondents expected consumer electronics to see price increases due to tariff policies on US trade partners.

A strategy businesses could deploy to reduce their exposure to added tariff-related costs is to bifurcate their hardware from embedded software, said Lynlee Brown, a partner in Ernst & Young's Global Trade practice.

Cook and NVIDIA Corp. CEO Jensen Huang have urged the Trump Administration to exempt their products from 145% tariffs imposed on Chinese imports. China dropped its tariffs on US microchips to zero, while maintaining its 125% retaliatory tariff on all other US imports.

Staying agile

Beyond tariffs, chipmakers are also navigating US policies that limit the sale of AI chips to China. In April, NVIDIA (NVDA) said it would record a write-down of up to $5.5 billion in its fiscal first quarter in the face of export restrictions on its H20 chips for the Chinese market.

Huang has pressed for the US to curtail those regulations.

"China is a very large market. It's probably going to be a $50 billion AI market in two or three years," Huang told CNBC on May 6. "It would be a tremendous loss not to be able to address it as an American company."

Also in April, NVIDIA announced plans to commission more than a million square feet of manufacturing space to build and test NVIDIA Blackwell chips in Arizona and AI supercomputers in Texas.

On CNBC, Huang stressed the importance of staying "agile" as a company. "Whatever the policies are of the government, whatever's in the best interest of our country, we'll support," he said.

Agility, though, is a luxury that is easier for some companies to afford than others. Larger, high-tech companies with more margin room can absorb impacts better than smaller manufacturers, said Ross Meyercord, CEO of Propel Software.

"One of the meta lessons to be learned here between COVID-19 and tariffs and trade wars is that resiliency and optionality are critical," Meyercord said. "We, of course, have to be cost-effective through this, but we need to find a way to blend optionality and alternate sources, alternate approaches as a way to mitigate risk."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Xometry: Tariffs May Not Be As Bad As Initially Feared (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Xometry's (NASDAQ:XMTR) Q1 results were strong, and the company gave solid forward guidance, particularly given the uncertain macro environment. The full impact of tariffs likely won't be known for some time, though, and the fact that Xometry recently conducted layoffs suggests that Xometry is feeling at least some pressure. Even though Xometry has relatively low direct exposure to tariffs, its business could still suffer if overall manufacturing activity declines.

I previously suggested that the fundamentals of Xometry’s business remained resilient despite increased macro uncertainty. The imposition of unexpectedly high tariffs has created additional headwinds since then, though, and as a result, manufacturing sentiment in the US has plummeted. Looking beyond this, there could be a reshoring tailwind if tariffs remain in place. Regardless, I am willing to look through any near term headwinds, as the custom manufacturing market is a large opportunity and Xometry only has a tiny share.

Market Conditions

Xometry's first quarter commentary and full-year guidance suggest that tariff headwinds are fairly modest at the moment. This isn't necessarily surprising given that Xometry has 18 localized marketplaces across the US, Europe and Asia and the vast majority of demand in the US was already fulfilled domestically.

While Xometry has seen an incremental shift towards domestic sourcing, low-cost offshore manufacturing is expected to remain important for some customers, and Xometry believes it can help meet this demand with its global network.

The full impact of tariffs likely won't be known for some time, but survey data already suggests a sharp decline in manufacturing activity in the US could be imminent. This represents a downside risk to Xometry's stock, given that it has more than fully recovered from the post "liberation day" drop.

Xometry Business Updates

The growth of Xometry's core business is being supported by the continued expansion of instant quoting capabilities. For example, Instant Quoting for Injection Molding was recently introduced in the EU, UK and Turkey. The company also plans on further adding to its Instant Quoting capabilities in 2025.

Xometry also continues to improve its Teamspace software, which is an important driver of adoption amongst enterprise customers. Teamspace is targeted at procurement teams managing assemblies and products, and appears to be broadly adopted amongst Xometry's larger customers. On the other side of the market, Xometry also continues to improve Workcenter, its cloud-based manufacturing execution system.

While Xometry's Marketplace continues to thrive, its Supplier Services business is languishing. Xometry is testing a new search experience on Thomasnet, which appears to be semantic search leveraging LLMs. The company also plans on introducing a new ad server, which will increase the inventory of advertising that can be sold on Thomasnet. Xometry appears to be facing a demand problem at the moment though, and it is not clear that these changes will help to address this.

Financial Analysis

Xometry generated 151 million USD revenue in Q1, an increase of 23% YoY. While this growth is strong, it came against a relatively easy comparable period in 2024. Marketplace revenue increased 27% to 136 million USD, driven by enterprise adoption, while Supplier Services revenue was down 6% YoY. Supplier Services was up sequentially though on the back of supplier financing. US marketplace growth reached nearly 30%, compared to 20% international growth. While international growth has decelerated, Xometry is still targeting 30-40% marketplace revenue contribution from international markets, compared to 18% in 2024.

Xometry expects 155-157 million USD revenue in Q2, an increase of 17-18% YoY. Marketplace revenue is expected to increase 20-22%, while Supplier Services revenue is expected to decline 5-7%. On the back of strong Q1 growth, Xometry also raised full-year guidance, and now anticipates 20-22% Marketplace growth, with Supplier Services revenue declining 5%.

The number of active buyers increased 22% YoY in the first quarter, reaching 71,454. Revenue per active buyer was also up 4% YoY, driven by enterprise adoption in the US. The number of accounts spending over 50,000 USD over the past 12 months increased 12% YoY. This is a positive given the weakness observed amongst this cohort in the fourth quarter of 2024.

Xometry's gross margin was 37.3% in the first quarter, with the sequential decline driven by a combination of mix shift and lower Marketplace gross margin. The dip in marketplace margin was expected due to investments in international markets. Marketplace gross margin is expected to rebound towards 34% in Q2.

While Xometry's gross margin dipped in the first quarter, revenue outperformance led to an improved adjusted EBITDA margin. Non-GAAP operating expenses increased 2% YoY to 56.4 million USD, driven by a reduction in sales and marketing expenses. As a result, Xometry's adjusted EBITDA was 100,000 USD in the quarter. This excludes 1.5 million USD of restructuring costs related to cost-cutting efforts that included a 5% workforce reduction.

Conclusion

Xometry's first quarter results and forward guidance were solid, helping to allay some of the concerns around potential tariff headwinds. The fallout won't really be known for some time, though, meaning it is still possible that Xometry's business slows in coming quarters. I don't believe that this is particularly concerning, though, due to the long-term potential of the business.

Xometry is targeting a large and fragmented market that it is still predominantly offline. It also continues to gain market share, which is important given that the competitive positioning of its marketplace should improve with scale.

Xometry is also still reasonably valued, despite its share price moving significantly higher over the past year. Xometry has the potential to generate solid double-digit revenue growth long-term and is targeting a 20% adjusted EBITDA margin target. As a result, I can see earnings growth driving Xometry's PE ratio into the single digits over the next 5-10 years, which should lead to strong returns.

data.title: DocuSign: Still The Most Dominant Digital Signature Software Company
data.description:
By Anthony Goh, Senior Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments

In this coverage, we analyzed DocuSign, Inc. (NASDAQ:DOCU), one of the top software providers in the global digital signature market, which has had a stellar 5-year revenue CAGR of 25.04% but now has a 3-year forward analysts’ consensus of only 7.4%. We investigate the sharp slowdown in the company’s revenue growth over the past two years and assess whether a recovery is likely. We start by analyzing whether the slowdown is tied to broader weakness in the global eSignature market. We then assess whether the company has lost competitiveness by comparing its flagship software with products from the leading players by market share. We also study the company’s business metrics, focusing on customer growth and average selling price trends, to understand how much they have contributed to the slowdown. Based on these findings, we forecast the company’s revenue growth prospects.

Macro Environment Weakness

In the first section we examined whether a factor for the company’s growth slowdown, which as seen in the table above, could be the result of the global eSignature market weakness given that the company claimed in its previous earnings transcript regarding “a tough macro environment” and that “companies are scrutinizing investments” which “leads to smaller expansion opportunities”. To verify management’s claim, we examine the bigger environment by compiling the global internet penetration rate, technology adoption rate in smartphones and PCs, and world globalization rate.

The chart above from KOF shows the globalization index for the world and the US, indicating a plateauing trend over the past decade since the 2010s, which, we believe, could affect demand growth for digital contract management and electronic signatures tied to international transactions.

We believe that the internet penetration rate acts as a major driver for the company, given that as internet access becomes more widespread, more businesses and individuals can engage in remote transactions, using services like DocuSign for secure digital signatures on contracts and official documents, which are often shared and signed via email. Based on the above, we compiled the global internet penetration percentage by dividing the global internet users by the world population. In 2024, the growth of the global internet penetration rate significantly slowed from 7.3% in 2022 to a flattish 1% in 2024, primarily due to a sharp slowdown in internet user growth from 8.14% in 2022 to 1.85% in 2024. We believe that the slowdown in internet penetration could be due to a market growth slowdown across developed countries, where a large portion of the national population already has access to the internet. According to DataReportal, the US internet penetration rate as of 2024 has already reached 97.1%, while other developed countries such as the UK and Germany also show a high internet penetration rate at 97.8% and 93.3% respectively.

We believe PC penetration is another key driver, given that increased PC penetration would allow more users in digital signature, such as in professional settings, to use DocuSign's platform in facilitating secure signing and management of business documents and contracts that are typically stored and conducted on PCs. Based on the above, there has been a growth slowdown in PC penetration over the years and amid that, PC market shipments have been weak with an average decline of -8.7% in the past 3 years. We believe this is attributable to market saturation of traditional PCs across developed countries, where a large portion of the national population already owns a PC, leading to fewer first-time buyers and slower growth. According to IBIS World, 95.7% of US households have at least one computer in 2023, while other developed regions such as Canada had a PC penetration of 83.9% in 2020 according to ITU Datahub.

We believe the smartphone penetration rate is also a key driver, as individuals can use smartphones to execute agreements and observed that the growth of the global smartphone penetration rate has also been consistently slowing down to a flattish growth averaging 3.6% over the past 3 years. We overall believe the company’s revenue slowdown may be partly explained by the maturing growth of the internet and the penetration rates. Based on our forecasts, we project internet, smartphone, and PC penetration growth rates to remain flattish due to these fundamental factors.

Competitiveness Software Features but at a Premium Pricing

We move on to assess whether the company’s revenue slowdown is driven by a loss of competitiveness in the global digital eSignature software market.

We compiled market share data from Datanyze and find that DocuSign’s share declined by 8.8% over the year, while competitors such as Xink, Adobe Sign (ADBE), Nitro, and SignNow gained between 0.6% and 3.5% share, highlighting rising competition for DocuSign from smaller companies, but it still leads with a solid market share of 68.2%, still a strong market position. To see how DocuSign software stacks against competitors, we compiled data including pricing, features and calculated a popularity score based on Gartner reviews.

From our comparison table above, DocuSign stands out in terms of popularity, as it has the highest score among competitors. We believe this reflects its first-mover advantage, with the company founded earlier than its competitors in 2003, compared to Xink in 2009 and Adobe, which only entered the market with EchoSign in 2011. We find that Adobe has a slight edge over DocuSign in terms of software feature count, though DocuSign is well ahead of smaller companies with a gap of 30 features. In terms of overall ranking, despite DocuSign’s strengths, our analysis shows Adobe comes out slightly ahead, as Adobe’s software is priced much lower than DocuSign’s, which has a premium subscription that may negatively affect demand from price-sensitive customers.

Considering pricing, features, and popularity rankings, we derive a total competitiveness score of 1.08x for the company and expect it to maintain a strong position in the digital signature software market, but we note rising competition from smaller players and premium pricing may pose challenges, still we believe its value offering is supported by feature-rich software. Management gave an insight into these developments in the latest earnings, stating that its product roadmap for FY2026 includes “Agreement AI, innovative new workflows, an expanded ecosystem, and powerful new capabilities for enterprise customers”. To assess whether it can continue enhancing its value proposition through feature development and R&D, we examine its financials in the next section.

Growth Driven by Expanding Customer Base

To further analyze the company’s growth performance, we examine its business metrics, such as customer and ASP growth, and compile the data in the table below for comparison with its overall growth performance.

Based on the above, we compiled the company’s customer base breakdown into enterprise and commercial customers and derived its non-enterprise customer count (individual users). We estimated ASP for enterprise and non-enterprise customers using a pricing ratio of 1 to 4.33 between business and individual plans, then calculated the respective pricing using the Excel Solver function. We observe that enterprise and commercial customer growth slowed more sharply, from 24.1% in FY2023 to 14.7% in FY2024 and further to 7.4% in FY2025. In comparison, non-enterprise customer growth also slowed but at a more moderate pace, from 17.1% in FY2023 to 15.5% in FY2024 and 14.5% in FY2025. Since FY2020, the revenue contribution from enterprise customers increased from 37.1% to 43.9% in FY2025, while the share from non-enterprise customers declined from 62.9% to 56.1%.

We observe that despite slower growth rates, the company has consistently added around 200,000 customers per year over the past three years. We believe that as DocuSign scales, sustaining high growth becomes increasingly difficult due to its already large market penetration, making new growth opportunities harder to capture. The percentage growth in customer base has declined even though absolute gains remain significant, which we believe reflects the maturity of its core markets. We also believe the ongoing slowdown in customer and ASP growth is closely linked to broader macro trends, as discussed earlier, with internet, PC, and smartphone penetration plateauing across major developed regions such as the US, UK, Germany, and Canada.

As seen in the chart, the company has paid down its debt over the past 3 years, improving its cash-to-debt ratio to 7.7x. We believe this strong financial position provides opportunities for future acquisitions, which could support further feature development and enhance competitiveness in the global digital signature market. The company has made a series of acquisitions in the past, such as SpringCM, Seal Software, and Clause, mainly to enhance its capabilities in document management, AI, and workflow automation, and we believe its recent acquisition in 2024 of Lexion could strengthen its feature set and boost its competitiveness. From its latest earnings, management gave a hint by highlighting the “integrated additional powerful Agreement AI capabilities” from Lexion, which give benefits as “IAM customers have reduced their contracting cycles by up to 75%”.

Based on the above, we project the company’s total customer base to increase by a consistent 190,000 per year through 2028, based on its past 7-year average. For ASP, which declined in FY2025, we conservatively forecast it using the past 3-year average of -2.91%, resulting in a projected 3-year forward average revenue growth of 6.9%. Our FY2026 forecast is also in line with management’s guidance of 5% YoY growth from its latest transcript. We also believe it is worth noting the company’s improved financial position, which could provide future additional growth opportunities for M&A and R&D investments to enhance its digital signature software features and overall value to customers.

Risk: Low Barriers to Entry of Software Market

According to the Canvas Business Model, the global eSignature market has “low barriers to entry due to technological advancements and the proliferation of cloud computing,” and the initial investment to launch a digital signature business can be relatively modest, often estimated at up to $50,000 for a basic software product. We believe this indicates a notable threat of new entrants, which could lead to increased competition and potential price pressure, especially given DocuSign’s relatively expensive pricing as discussed in the second point.

Verdict

We believe DocuSign’s recent revenue slowdown compared to its past may be due to the plateauing of key adoption drivers, including internet, PC, and smartphone penetration, which we expect to remain flat over the next few years. But despite this, we still believe DocuSign maintains a solid competitive position, supported by its strong feature set, though we note increasing pressure from rising competition and its premium pricing. We project an average revenue growth of 6.9% over the next three years, which, we believe, is in line with management’s FY2026 guidance of 5% growth and consistent with the 3-year forward analysts’ consensus. We also believe DocuSign’s strengthened financial position, with the paired debt and increased cash, could enable it to pursue future acquisitions and invest in R&D, such as the 2024 acquisition of Lexion to enhance its AI capabilities. We thus believe DocuSign could stay competitive in the digital signature market.

For our valuation of the company, we used a DCF model, as it has positive FCF margins (5-year average of 22.8%). While revenue growth has slowed, we expect stable revenue growth averaging 5.7% annually over the next five years. We also note that profitability could drive further upside, with operating margins improving from -49% in 2015 to 7.8% in 2024, and we project this to rise to 20.45% by 2029 as the company scales. Using a discount rate of 10.9% (the company’s WACC) and a terminal value based on the top software industry companies' 5-year average EV/EBITDA of 27.62x, our model indicates an upside of 11.49%. With the stock down 9.2% YTD, we rate the company as a Buy.
